humanities_role_explanation: MMLU includes specific academic humanities subjects (e.g., history, philosophy, world religions) as target evaluation domains while using their textual questions as content to assess general multi-task language understanding and reasoning.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only multiple-choice questions with four options, processed in zero-shot and few-shot text prompts.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates factual recognition of historical and religious events, logical deduction in formal logic, moral reasoning in everyday scenarios, and legal rule application in professional law cases.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates broad language understanding across 57 diverse subjects spanning STEM, humanities, and social sciences.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates GPT-3 (ranging from Ada to Davinci/175B) and GPT-2.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs and text-to-text models (UnifiedQA) are the primary focus of the evaluation.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes datasets for History (US, World, European), Philosophy, Religious Studies (World Religions), and Jurisprudence/Law.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluated primarily via multi-choice classification accuracy, alongside calibration error metrics.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published at ICLR in 2021 (preprinted in late 2020).

english_language.note: The paper and all questions in the benchmark are written in English.

summary: This paper introduces the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark, a test spanning 57 subjects designed to measure LLM multitask knowledge and reasoning. The dataset contains 15,908 multiple-choice questions covering elementary to professional difficulty levels. It evaluates models in zero-shot and few-shot transfer settings to assess real-world knowledge acquisition from pretraining.
key_findings: The largest GPT-3 model (175B) achieved 43.9% average accuracy, scoring far above random chance (25%) but well below the expert human estimate of 89.8%. Smaller models performed close to random baseline. LLM capabilities were found to be highly lopsided, showing weakness in calculation-heavy STEM subjects, morality, and law, while exhibiting poor calibration (unaware of when they are wrong).
